items.title: Human cooperation is strong among individuals but fragile between groups
items.author: Paul K. Bergmann
items.content: There is ample empirical evidence that humans are cooperative, but there is also evidence that humans can be distrustful, hostile and aggressive. In this Review, Van Lange and Bergmann reconcile this conflicting evidence by arguing that cooperation is more easily achieved between individuals, especially in dyads, than between groups.

items.title: Migrants’ and local residents’ psychological responses to migration
items.author: Julia C. Becker
items.content: Migration transforms migrants’ material, social, and cultural environments. Migration also changes the receiving context, which affects local residents. In this Review, Echterhoff et al. consider the interplay between migrants’ and local residents’ responses to migration and the influence of the perceived forced nature of migration.

items.title: The costs of incremental research in cognitive psychology
items.author: Julie M. Bugg
items.content: Decades of research has aimed to distinguish cognitive control from bottom-up learning and memory processes. Despite early consensus that both mechanisms contribute to key phenomena, debates persist. We argue that battles like these reach a point of diminishing returns, consuming substantial time, effort, and resources without further advancing theoretical understanding.

items.title: The psychology of eating requires social context
items.author: Jutta Mata
items.content: Beyond meeting a basic need, eating behaviour shapes human health and environmental outcomes. In this Review, Mata highlights the fundamentally social nature of eating and describes how to integrate this evidence into current research on behaviour change.

items.title: Transition dynamics of external and internal attention across on-task and off-task states
items.author: Matthias Mittner
items.content: Attention can be flexibly directed to information that is on- or off-task and internal or external to the individual. In this Perspective, Verschooren and colleagues bring together behavioural and neuromodulatory evidence to propose a unified space of attentional states and transitions between them.
